trajectory_summary: The call moved from neutral cooperation through a cost-curiosity spike, a scam-anxiety dip, and landed on conditional commitment — Casey agreed to send the bill before an appointment is scheduled. Holland kept the call alive through a driving constraint and a dead-zone warning, which is competent field work. However, the outcome is structurally fragile: no appointment is booked, the bill has not been received, and the scam concern was parked rather than resolved. If this is a paid lead, the codex's bill_collection rules mean the appointment cannot be set until the bill arrives — making the follow-through rate the only thing that determines whether this call produces a sit.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the correct classification is 'conditional_callback': Casey agreed to send a bill screenshot, after which Holland indicated an appointment would be scheduled. No appointment was booked on the call. Whether this converts to a sit depends entirely on Casey following through with the bill, which was not locked with a specific timeframe or a button-up sequence.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Conditional close — locking a time anchor before hanging up
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'll just send you that text. I'll look forward to that usage from you, and then we can, schedule an appointment after that.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland correctly pivoted on the driving constraint but let the call end with zero scheduling structure. No time preference captured, no 'is that fair' handshake, no urgency signal. The outcome is entirely dependent on Casey voluntarily sending a bill screenshot with no deadline. Per the comp model, Holland gets paid on sits — a soft callback with no time anchor is the highest-risk outcome on a cooperative lead. This is the single most actionable gap from this call: Holland had Casey's agreement and didn't close the loop.

key_moments.quote: did you get, like, a lot of trees removed and stuff?
key_moments.stewart_read: Casey confirmed tree removal, which materially improves sun exposure and system sizing potential. Holland noted it ('Okay. That's what I thought') and moved on without anchoring it as a positive signal for Casey's design. This was an opportunity to build curiosity: the cleared roof exposure is exactly why the design looks promising.

key_moments.quote: and about a $100 of that is the payment for coal. I have to pay the pole and electricity ran.
key_moments.stewart_read: Casey disclosed that $100/month of the $300–$400 bill is a fixed pole-and-infrastructure charge — money paid regardless of consumption. This is a sharper bill-pain anchor than the total bill alone: it's a sunk cost Casey cannot reduce by conserving energy. Holland acknowledged it ('Yeah. That's definitely good to know') and moved on without flipping it. The flip was available: the pole charge is the clearest example of paying Southside Electric for nothing solar can't replace.

key_moments.quote: And plus, like, if for some reason the solar panels were to go down, I wouldn't have to run off generator power.
key_moments.stewart_read: Casey named backup power as an explicit concern — this is a Tesla Powerwall cross-sell signal. Holland responded only to the net metering dimension ('Yeah. No problem. We can definitely do that for you') without surfacing the Powerwall as the product that directly addresses backup-power anxiety. Ion carries the Tesla Powerwall as its only battery brand, and this customer's concern maps directly to it.

key_moments.quote: I mean, you feel like a young person too, and you know all the scams and stuff out there.
key_moments.stewart_read: Casey named scam anxiety directly and framed it as a shared generational experience — a trust gift. Holland's response ('I've had my fair share with scams. I'm just as scared of those as anybody else') was personally empathetic but missed the structural legitimacy anchors available: Casey filled out a form (customer-initiated), Ion is Tesla's certified installer partner in Virginia, and the appointment itself is free with no commitment. The personal empathy parked the concern; structural anchors would have resolved it.
